content.text: <ide_opened_file>The user opened the file /Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/attacks.py in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>

add to attacks.py a v0 general adversarial agent that creates negative output examples trying to compose an adversarial output that scores high on the rubric, but would score low on an empirical evaluation / pairwise comparison with a gold example. it should use the same minimal ffmpeg-centric harness as the rollout code plus extra relevant tools (eg it also has access to the golden example it can clone and corrupt). upon impl, test and evaluate the quality of adversarial examples it generates compared to the mechanically generated ones

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Build adversarial agent for attacks.py evaluation

content.text: I'll start by exploring the repo to understand the existing harness, rollout code, rubric, and current attacks.py.

content.content:
1	"""Mechanically-generated reward-hacking deliverables for Axis 2 (hackability).
2	
3	Where `null_inputs` builds *do-nothing* outputs (a well-calibrated rubric should
4	score them ~0), this builds *cheap-but-targeted* attacks: deliverables that try to
5	raise the rubric score WITHOUT doing the real work. They are the empirical core of
6	the hackability axis — the "attack suite" the iterator scores a rubric against.
7	
8	An attack passing a positive item is a `leak` (run_common.leaks): the item rewards
9	surface form, not quality, and is a rewrite target. Because the attacks are purely
10	mechanical (no judge/policy LLM in the loop) they are free, deterministic, and
11	reproducible with a single command — the same contract as null_inputs.
12	
13	Attack families
14	---------------
15	restate_input   (documents)  Echo the brief: dump the PROMPT plus every reference
16	                             file's text into the deliverable type, under generic
17	                             headers, with no analysis. Probes "the answer is in
18	                             the brief" — items satisfiable by parroting inputs.
19	
20	keyword_stuff   (documents)  Weaponize the rubric against itself: emit a document
21	                             containing every quoted literal the rubric demands
22	                             (section labels, identifiers, header fields) and the
23	                             de-verbed clause of every criterion ("States that X"
24	                             -> "X"). The rubric becomes its own answer key. Items
25	                             it passes are pure surface-match hacks (presence-only,
26	                             self-certification, literal-string).
27	
28	presence_max    (video)      Max out cheap presence cues: a long black/source video
29	                             with a giant burned-in title and a caption bar covering
30	                             the frame. Probes presence-without-absence ("caption is
31	                             visible" satisfied by a caption that hides the subject).
32	
33	stems_named     (archive)    A stems zip with the right-looking filenames (mined from
34	                             the rubric/refs) but only silence. Probes "includes an
35	                             X stem / the stems are present" presence items.
36	
37	Each attack is task-aware but generated from generic, type-keyed rules, so the suite
38	extends to the gold-free tasks too (deliverable type is inferred from the rubric when
39	no gold is released).
40	"""
41	from __future__ import annotations
42	
43	import json
44	import re
45	import shutil
46	from pathlib import Path
47	
48	import fitz
49	
50	from . import config
51	from .deliverable import extract_text, kind
52	from .null_inputs import _black_video, _run, _silent_wav, _zip_of
53	
54	# Leading instruction phrases stripped from a criterion to turn it from a check
55	# ("States in the report that the subject left at 7:45") into an assertion the
56	# document can carry verbatim ("the subject left at 7:45"). Longest-first so the
57	# more specific phrasings win.
58	_VERB_PREFIXES = sorted([
59	    "states in the report that", "states in the summary section that",
60	    "explicitly states in the assessment section that", "explicitly states that",
61	    "affirms in the report that", "confirms in the report that",
62	    "mentions in the report that", "notes in the report that",
63	    "mentions photographic evidence of", "states in the report",
64	    "contains a section in the report labeled", "contains a section labeled",
65	    "produces the sections in the following order:", "lists the case type as",
66	    "lists the case identifier", "lists the investigator name as",
67	    "lists the assignment start date as", "lists the subject vehicle as",
68	    "lists the surveillance location as", "lists the client identifier",
69	    "lists the", "provides the deliverable as", "provides a", "provides",
70	    "ends the report with", "refers to the unknown man as",
71	    "states that", "states", "affirms", "confirms", "mentions", "notes",
72	    "contains", "includes", "the report states that",
73	], key=len, reverse=True)
74	
75	# Keywords that hint at the deliverable type when no gold is released.
76	_KIND_HINTS = {
77	    "document": ("pdf", "report", "memo", "document", "write-up", "summary section"),
78	    "video": ("video", "mp4", ".mov", "footage", "edit", "cut", "render", "timeline",
79	              "caption", "title card", "lower third"),
80	    "archive": ("stem", "stems", "zip", "mixdown", "mix-down", "bounce", "wav",
81	                "audio file", "track", "session"),
82	}
83	# Fallback by occupation when the rubric is ambiguous.
84	_OCC_DEFAULT = {
85	    "private_detectives_and_investigators": "document",
86	    "producers_and_directors": "document",
87	    "film_and_video_editors": "video",
88	    "audio_and_video_technicians": "archive",
89	}
90	
91	# --- quoted-literal / clause mining -----------------------------------------
92	_QUOTE_RE = re.compile(r"[\"'‘’“”]([^\"'‘’“”]{2,90}?)"
93	                       r"[\"'‘’“”]")
94	
95	
96	def _task(task_folder: str) -> dict:
97	    return json.loads((config.DATA / task_folder / "task.json").read_text())
98	
99	
100	def infer_deliverable_kind(task_folder: str) -> str:
101	    """Target deliverable type: gold if released, else inferred from the rubric/prompt."""
102	    meta = _task(task_folder)
103	    dels = meta.get("deliverable_files") or []
104	    if dels:
105	        return kind(dels[0]["name"])
106	    text = (meta.get("prompt", "") + " " + _rubric_text(task_folder)).lower()
107	    scores = {k: sum(text.count(h) for h in hints) for k, hints in _KIND_HINTS.items()}
108	    best = max(scores, key=scores.get)
109	    if scores[best] == 0:
110	        return _OCC_DEFAULT.get(meta.get("occupation", ""), "document")
111	    return best
112	
113	
114	def _rubric_items(task_folder: str) -> list[dict]:
115	    p = config.DATA / task_folder / "rubric.json"
116	    return json.loads(p.read_text()) if p.exists() else []
117	
118	
119	def _rubric_text(task_folder: str) -> str:
120	    return "\n".join(i.get("criterion", "") for i in _rubric_items(task_folder))
121	
122	
123	def _deverb(criterion: str) -> str:
124	    """Strip a leading instruction verb so the fact reads as an assertion."""
125	    c = criterion.strip()
126	    low = c.lower()
127	    for pre in _VERB_PREFIXES:
128	        if low.startswith(pre):
129	            rest = c[len(pre):].strip(" :,-")
130	            return rest or c
131	    return c
132	
133	
134	def _quoted_literals(task_folder: str) -> list[str]:
135	    """Exact strings the rubric demands: section labels, ids, header field values."""
136	    seen, out = set(), []
137	    for it in _rubric_items(task_folder):
138	        for m in _QUOTE_RE.findall(it.get("criterion", "")):
139	            s = m.strip()
140	            if s and s.lower() not in seen:
141	                seen.add(s.lower())
142	                out.append(s)
143	    return out
144	
145	
146	def _reference_texts(task_folder: str) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
147	    meta = _task(task_folder)
148	    out = []
149	    rdir = config.DATA / task_folder / "reference_files"
150	    for r in meta.get("reference_files", []):
151	        p = rdir / Path(r["name"]).name
152	        if p.exists() and p.suffix.lower() in (".docx", ".pdf", ".txt", ".md", ".srt"):
153	            out.append((p.name, extract_text(p)))
154	    return out
155	
156	
157	# --- document rendering -----------------------------------------------------
158	def _sectioned_pdf(out: Path, sections: list[tuple[str, str]]) -> None:
159	    """Render (heading, body) sections across as many pages as needed."""
160	    doc = fitz.open()
161	    margin, top, lh = 50, 60, 11
162	    page = doc.new_page()
163	    y = top
164	    width = page.rect.width - 2 * margin
165	
166	    def newpage():
167	        nonlocal page, y
168	        page = doc.new_page()
169	        y = top
170	
171	    for heading, body in sections:
172	        if y > page.rect.height - 90:
173	            newpage()
174	        page.insert_text((margin, y), heading, fontsize=14, fontname="helv")
175	        y += lh * 2
176	        for line in (body or "").splitlines():
177	            for chunk in _wrap(line, width):
178	                if y > page.rect.height - margin:
179	                    newpage()
180	                page.insert_text((margin, y), chunk, fontsize=9, fontname="helv")
181	                y += lh
182	        y += lh
183	    doc.save(out)
184	
185	
186	def _wrap(line: str, width: float, charw: float = 5.0) -> list[str]:
187	    maxc = max(20, int(width / charw))
188	    if len(line) <= maxc:
189	        return [line] if line else [""]
190	    words, cur, out = line.split(), "", []
191	    for w in words:
192	        if len(cur) + len(w) + 1 > maxc:
193	            out.append(cur)
194	            cur = w
195	        else:
196	            cur = f"{cur} {w}".strip()
197	    if cur:
198	        out.append(cur)
199	    return out
200	
201	
202	def _restate_input_pdf(task_folder: str, out: Path) -> None:
203	    meta = _task(task_folder)
204	    secs = [("Summary", meta.get("prompt", ""))]
205	    for name, text in _reference_texts(task_folder):
206	        secs.append((f"Reference: {name}", text[:8000]))
207	    _sectioned_pdf(out, secs)
208	
209	
210	def _keyword_stuff_pdf(task_folder: str, out: Path) -> None:
211	    literals = _quoted_literals(task_folder)
212	    clauses = [_deverb(it["criterion"]) for it in _rubric_items(task_folder)
213	               if float(it.get("score", 0)) > 0]
214	    secs: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
215	    # Use any quoted section labels as headings up front (e.g. Summary/Surveillance).
216	    for lit in literals:
217	        secs.append((lit, ""))
218	    secs.append(("Header", "\n".join(literals)))
219	    secs.append(("Findings", "\n".join(clauses)))
220	    # Plus echo the brief so indirectly-stated facts are covered too.
221	    secs.append(("Brief", _task(task_folder).get("prompt", "")[:6000]))
222	    _sectioned_pdf(out, secs)
223	
224	
225	# --- media rendering --------------------------------------------------------
226	_MAC_FONTS = [
227	    "/System/Library/Fonts/Supplemental/Arial.ttf",
228	    "/System/Library/Fonts/Helvetica.ttc",
229	    "/Library/Fonts/Arial.ttf",
230	]
231	
232	
233	def _font() -> str | None:
234	    return next((f for f in _MAC_FONTS if Path(f).exists()), None)
235	
236	
237	def _presence_max_video(task_folder: str, out: Path, dur: int = 60) -> None:
238	    """Long video with an oversized burned-in title + caption bar covering the frame.
239	
240	    Falls back to a plain long black video if drawtext is unavailable, which still
241	    probes duration/'a video was produced' presence items.
242	    """
243	    refs = _task(task_folder).get("reference_files", [])
244	    rdir = config.DATA / task_folder / "reference_files"
245	    src = next((rdir / Path(r["name"]).name for r in refs
246	                if Path(r["name"]).suffix.lower() in (".mp4", ".mov")), None)
247	    title = (_quoted_literals(task_folder) or ["TITLE"])[0][:40].replace(":", " ")
248	    font = _font()
249	    base = ["ffmpeg", "-y"]
250	    if src and src.exists():
251	        base += ["-stream_loop", "-1", "-i", str(src), "-t", str(dur)]
252	    else:
253	        base += ["-f", "lavfi", "-i", f"color=c=black:s=854x480:d={dur}",
254	                 "-f", "lavfi", "-i", "anullsrc=r=16000:cl=mono", "-t", str(dur), "-shortest"]
255	    if font:
256	        vf = (f"drawtext=fontfile='{font}':text='{title}':fontsize=64:fontcolor=white:"
257	              f"x=(w-text_w)/2:y=h/3,"
258	              f"drawtext=fontfile='{font}':text='CAPTION — CAPTION — CAPTION':fontsize=48:"
259	              f"fontcolor=yellow:box=1:boxcolor=black@0.8:x=(w-text_w)/2:y=h-120")
260	        cmd = base + ["-vf", vf, "-pix_fmt", "yuv420p", str(out)]
261	        try:
262	            _run(cmd)
263	            return
264	        except Exception:
265	            pass  # drawtext unavailable -> fall through to plain long video
266	    _run(base + ["-pix_fmt", "yuv420p", str(out)])
267	
268	
269	def _stem_names(task_folder: str) -> list[str]:
270	    """Plausible stem filenames mined from the rubric, with sane defaults."""
271	    text = _rubric_text(task_folder).lower()
272	    cands = ["vocal", "lead_vocal", "drums", "bass", "guitar", "keys", "synth",
273	             "percussion", "backing_vocal", "fx", "master", "mixdown"]
274	    named = [c for c in cands if c.replace("_", " ") in text or c in text]
275	    return [f"{n}.wav" for n in (named or ["lead_vocal", "drums", "bass", "master"])]
276	
277	
278	def _stems_named_zip(task_folder: str, out_dir: Path) -> None:
279	    tmp = out_dir / "_silence.wav"
280	    _silent_wav(tmp, dur=10)
281	    members = []
282	    for name in _stem_names(task_folder):
283	        p = out_dir / name
284	        shutil.copy(tmp, p)
285	        members.append(p)
286	    _zip_of(members, out_dir / "stems.zip")
287	    for p in members + [tmp]:
288	        p.unlink()
289	
290	
291	# --- driver -----------------------------------------------------------------
292	def build_attacks(task_folder: str) -> dict[str, Path]:
293	    """Materialize the attack deliverables on disk; return {attack_name: directory}."""
294	    dk = infer_deliverable_kind(task_folder)
295	    base = config.ATTACKS / task_folder
296	    variants: dict[str, Path] = {}
297	
298	    def mkdir(name: str) -> Path:
299	        d = base / name
300	        if d.exists():
301	            shutil.rmtree(d)
302	        d.mkdir(parents=True)
303	        return d
304	
305	    if dk == "document":
306	        d = mkdir("restate_input"); _restate_input_pdf(task_folder, d / "restate_input.pdf")
307	        variants["restate_input"] = d
308	        d = mkdir("keyword_stuff"); _keyword_stuff_pdf(task_folder, d / "keyword_stuff.pdf")
309	        variants["keyword_stuff"] = d
310	
311	    elif dk == "video":
312	        d = mkdir("presence_max")
313	        _presence_max_video(task_folder, d / "presence_max.mp4")
314	        variants["presence_max"] = d
315	
316	    elif dk == "archive":
317	        d = mkdir("stems_named"); _stems_named_zip(task_folder, d)
318	        variants["stems_named"] = d
319	
320	    return variants
321	
322	
323	def attack_names(task_folder: str) -> list[str]:
324	    """Attack subject names available for a task (without materializing files)."""
325	    return {"document": ["restate_input", "keyword_stuff"],
326	            "video": ["presence_max"],
327	            "archive": ["stems_named"]}.get(infer_deliverable_kind(task_folder), [])
328	
329	
330	if __name__ == "__main__":
331	    import sys
332	    for tf in sys.argv[1:] or [p.name for p in config.DATA.iterdir() if p.is_dir()]:
333	        v = build_attacks(tf)
334	        print(f"{tf}: [{infer_deliverable_kind(tf)}] -> {list(v) or '(none)'}")
335

"""Mechanically-generated reward-hacking deliverables for Axis 2 (hackability).

Where `null_inputs` builds *do-nothing* outputs (a well-calibrated rubric should
score them ~0), this builds *cheap-but-targeted* attacks: deliverables that try to
raise the rubric score WITHOUT doing the real work. They are the empirical core of
the hackability axis — the "attack suite" the iterator scores a rubric against.

An attack passing a positive item is a `leak` (run_common.leaks): the item rewards
surface form, not quality, and is a rewrite target. Because the attacks are purely
mechanical (no judge/policy LLM in the loop) they are free, deterministic, and
reproducible with a single command — the same contract as null_inputs.

Attack families
---------------
restate_input   (documents)  Echo the brief: dump the PROMPT plus every reference
                             file's text into the deliverable type, under generic
                             headers, with no analysis. Probes "the answer is in
                             the brief" — items satisfiable by parroting inputs.

keyword_stuff   (documents)  Weaponize the rubric against itself: emit a document
                             containing every quoted literal the rubric demands
                             (section labels, identifiers, header fields) and the
                             de-verbed clause of every criterion ("States that X"
                             -> "X"). The rubric becomes its own answer key. Items
                             it passes are pure surface-match hacks (presence-only,
                             self-certification, literal-string).

presence_max    (video)      Max out cheap presence cues: a long black/source video
                             with a giant burned-in title and a caption bar covering
                             the frame. Probes presence-without-absence ("caption is
                             visible" satisfied by a caption that hides the subject).

stems_named     (archive)    A stems zip with the right-looking filenames (mined from
                             the rubric/refs) but only silence. Probes "includes an
                             X stem / the stems are present" presence items.

Each attack is task-aware but generated from generic, type-keyed rules, so the suite
extends to the gold-free tasks too (deliverable type is inferred from the rubric when
no gold is released).
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import shutil
from pathlib import Path

import fitz

from . import config
from .deliverable import extract_text, kind
from .null_inputs import _black_video, _run, _silent_wav, _zip_of

# Leading instruction phrases stripped from a criterion to turn it from a check
# ("States in the report that the subject left at 7:45") into an assertion the
# document can carry verbatim ("the subject left at 7:45"). Longest-first so the
# more specific phrasings win.
_VERB_PREFIXES = sorted([
    "states in the report that", "states in the summary section that",
    "explicitly states in the assessment section that", "explicitly states that",
    "affirms in the report that", "confirms in the report that",
    "mentions in the report that", "notes in the report that",
    "mentions photographic evidence of", "states in the report",
    "contains a section in the report labeled", "contains a section labeled",
    "produces the sections in the following order:", "lists the case type as",
    "lists the case identifier", "lists the investigator name as",
    "lists the assignment start date as", "lists the subject vehicle as",
    "lists the surveillance location as", "lists the client identifier",
    "lists the", "provides the deliverable as", "provides a", "provides",
    "ends the report with", "refers to the unknown man as",
    "states that", "states", "affirms", "confirms", "mentions", "notes",
    "contains", "includes", "the report states that",
], key=len, reverse=True)

# Keywords that hint at the deliverable type when no gold is released.
_KIND_HINTS = {
    "document": ("pdf", "report", "memo", "document", "write-up", "summary section"),
    "video": ("video", "mp4", ".mov", "footage", "edit", "cut", "render", "timeline",
              "caption", "title card", "lower third"),
    "archive": ("stem", "stems", "zip", "mixdown", "mix-down", "bounce", "wav",
                "audio file", "track", "session"),
}
# Fallback by occupation when the rubric is ambiguous.
_OCC_DEFAULT = {
    "private_detectives_and_investigators": "document",
    "producers_and_directors": "document",
    "film_and_video_editors": "video",
    "audio_and_video_technicians": "archive",
}

# --- quoted-literal / clause mining -----------------------------------------
_QUOTE_RE = re.compile(r"[\"'‘’“”]([^\"'‘’“”]{2,90}?)"
                       r"[\"'‘’“”]")


def _task(task_folder: str) -> dict:
    return json.loads((config.DATA / task_folder / "task.json").read_text())


def infer_deliverable_kind(task_folder: str) -> str:
    """Target deliverable type: gold if released, else inferred from the rubric/prompt."""
    meta = _task(task_folder)
    dels = meta.get("deliverable_files") or []
    if dels:
        return kind(dels[0]["name"])
    text = (meta.get("prompt", "") + " " + _rubric_text(task_folder)).lower()
    scores = {k: sum(text.count(h) for h in hints) for k, hints in _KIND_HINTS.items()}
    best = max(scores, key=scores.get)
    if scores[best] == 0:
        return _OCC_DEFAULT.get(meta.get("occupation", ""), "document")
    return best


def _rubric_items(task_folder: str) -> list[dict]:
    p = config.DATA / task_folder / "rubric.json"
    return json.loads(p.read_text()) if p.exists() else []


def _rubric_text(task_folder: str) -> str:
    return "\n".join(i.get("criterion", "") for i in _rubric_items(task_folder))


def _deverb(criterion: str) -> str:
    """Strip a leading instruction verb so the fact reads as an assertion."""
    c = criterion.strip()
    low = c.lower()
    for pre in _VERB_PREFIXES:
        if low.startswith(pre):
            rest = c[len(pre):].strip(" :,-")
            return rest or c
    return c


def _quoted_literals(task_folder: str) -> list[str]:
    """Exact strings the rubric demands: section labels, ids, header field values."""
    seen, out = set(), []
    for it in _rubric_items(task_folder):
        for m in _QUOTE_RE.findall(it.get("criterion", "")):
            s = m.strip()
            if s and s.lower() not in seen:
                seen.add(s.lower())
                out.append(s)
    return out


def _reference_texts(task_folder: str) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    meta = _task(task_folder)
    out = []
    rdir = config.DATA / task_folder / "reference_files"
    for r in meta.get("reference_files", []):
        p = rdir / Path(r["name"]).name
        if p.exists() and p.suffix.lower() in (".docx", ".pdf", ".txt", ".md", ".srt"):
            out.append((p.name, extract_text(p)))
    return out


# --- document rendering -----------------------------------------------------
def _sectioned_pdf(out: Path, sections: list[tuple[str, str]]) -> None:
    """Render (heading, body) sections across as many pages as needed."""
    doc = fitz.open()
    margin, top, lh = 50, 60, 11
    page = doc.new_page()
    y = top
    width = page.rect.width - 2 * margin

    def newpage():
        nonlocal page, y
        page = doc.new_page()
        y = top

    for heading, body in sections:
        if y > page.rect.height - 90:
            newpage()
        page.insert_text((margin, y), heading, fontsize=14, fontname="helv")
        y += lh * 2
        for line in (body or "").splitlines():
            for chunk in _wrap(line, width):
                if y > page.rect.height - margin:
                    newpage()
                page.insert_text((margin, y), chunk, fontsize=9, fontname="helv")
                y += lh
        y += lh
    doc.save(out)


def _wrap(line: str, width: float, charw: float = 5.0) -> list[str]:
    maxc = max(20, int(width / charw))
    if len(line) <= maxc:
        return [line] if line else [""]
    words, cur, out = line.split(), "", []
    for w in words:
        if len(cur) + len(w) + 1 > maxc:
            out.append(cur)
            cur = w
        else:
            cur = f"{cur} {w}".strip()
    if cur:
        out.append(cur)
    return out


def _restate_input_pdf(task_folder: str, out: Path) -> None:
    meta = _task(task_folder)
    secs = [("Summary", meta.get("prompt", ""))]
    for name, text in _reference_texts(task_folder):
        secs.append((f"Reference: {name}", text[:8000]))
    _sectioned_pdf(out, secs)


def _keyword_stuff_pdf(task_folder: str, out: Path) -> None:
    literals = _quoted_literals(task_folder)
    clauses = [_deverb(it["criterion"]) for it in _rubric_items(task_folder)
               if float(it.get("score", 0)) > 0]
    secs: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    # Use any quoted section labels as headings up front (e.g. Summary/Surveillance).
    for lit in literals:
        secs.append((lit, ""))
    secs.append(("Header", "\n".join(literals)))
    secs.append(("Findings", "\n".join(clauses)))
    # Plus echo the brief so indirectly-stated facts are covered too.
    secs.append(("Brief", _task(task_folder).get("prompt", "")[:6000]))
    _sectioned_pdf(out, secs)


# --- media rendering --------------------------------------------------------
_MAC_FONTS = [
    "/System/Library/Fonts/Supplemental/Arial.ttf",
    "/System/Library/Fonts/Helvetica.ttc",
    "/Library/Fonts/Arial.ttf",
]


def _font() -> str | None:
    return next((f for f in _MAC_FONTS if Path(f).exists()), None)


def _presence_max_video(task_folder: str, out: Path, dur: int = 60) -> None:
    """Long video with an oversized burned-in title + caption bar covering the frame.

    Falls back to a plain long black video if drawtext is unavailable, which still
    probes duration/'a video was produced' presence items.
    """
    refs = _task(task_folder).get("reference_files", [])
    rdir = config.DATA / task_folder / "reference_files"
    src = next((rdir / Path(r["name"]).name for r in refs
                if Path(r["name"]).suffix.lower() in (".mp4", ".mov")), None)
    title = (_quoted_literals(task_folder) or ["TITLE"])[0][:40].replace(":", " ")
    font = _font()
    base = ["ffmpeg", "-y"]
    if src and src.exists():
        base += ["-stream_loop", "-1", "-i", str(src), "-t", str(dur)]
    else:
        base += ["-f", "lavfi", "-i", f"color=c=black:s=854x480:d={dur}",
                 "-f", "lavfi", "-i", "anullsrc=r=16000:cl=mono", "-t", str(dur), "-shortest"]
    if font:
        vf = (f"drawtext=fontfile='{font}':text='{title}':fontsize=64:fontcolor=white:"
              f"x=(w-text_w)/2:y=h/3,"
              f"drawtext=fontfile='{font}':text='CAPTION — CAPTION — CAPTION':fontsize=48:"
              f"fontcolor=yellow:box=1:boxcolor=black@0.8:x=(w-text_w)/2:y=h-120")
        cmd = base + ["-vf", vf, "-pix_fmt", "yuv420p", str(out)]
        try:
            _run(cmd)
            return
        except Exception:
            pass  # drawtext unavailable -> fall through to plain long video
    _run(base + ["-pix_fmt", "yuv420p", str(out)])


def _stem_names(task_folder: str) -> list[str]:
    """Plausible stem filenames mined from the rubric, with sane defaults."""
    text = _rubric_text(task_folder).lower()
    cands = ["vocal", "lead_vocal", "drums", "bass", "guitar", "keys", "synth",
             "percussion", "backing_vocal", "fx", "master", "mixdown"]
    named = [c for c in cands if c.replace("_", " ") in text or c in text]
    return [f"{n}.wav" for n in (named or ["lead_vocal", "drums", "bass", "master"])]


def _stems_named_zip(task_folder: str, out_dir: Path) -> None:
    tmp = out_dir / "_silence.wav"
    _silent_wav(tmp, dur=10)
    members = []
    for name in _stem_names(task_folder):
        p = out_dir / name
        shutil.copy(tmp, p)
        members.append(p)
    _zip_of(members, out_dir / "stems.zip")
    for p in members + [tmp]:
        p.unlink()


# --- driver -----------------------------------------------------------------
def build_attacks(task_folder: str) -> dict[str, Path]:
    """Materialize the attack deliverables on disk; return {attack_name: directory}."""
    dk = infer_deliverable_kind(task_folder)
    base = config.ATTACKS / task_folder
    variants: dict[str, Path] = {}

    def mkdir(name: str) -> Path:
        d = base / name
        if d.exists():
            shutil.rmtree(d)
        d.mkdir(parents=True)
        return d

    if dk == "document":
        d = mkdir("restate_input"); _restate_input_pdf(task_folder, d / "restate_input.pdf")
        variants["restate_input"] = d
        d = mkdir("keyword_stuff"); _keyword_stuff_pdf(task_folder, d / "keyword_stuff.pdf")
        variants["keyword_stuff"] = d

    elif dk == "video":
        d = mkdir("presence_max")
        _presence_max_video(task_folder, d / "presence_max.mp4")
        variants["presence_max"] = d

    elif dk == "archive":
        d = mkdir("stems_named"); _stems_named_zip(task_folder, d)
        variants["stems_named"] = d

    return variants


def attack_names(task_folder: str) -> list[str]:
    """Attack subject names available for a task (without materializing files)."""
    return {"document": ["restate_input", "keyword_stuff"],
            "video": ["presence_max"],
            "archive": ["stems_named"]}.get(infer_deliverable_kind(task_folder), [])


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    for tf in sys.argv[1:] or [p.name for p in config.DATA.iterdir() if p.is_dir()]:
        v = build_attacks(tf)
        print(f"{tf}: [{infer_deliverable_kind(tf)}] -> {list(v) or '(none)'}")

1	CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
2	PHILO LABS
3	Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
4	Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
5	~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
6	Context
7	Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
8	purpose.
9	For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
10	rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
11	The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
12	every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
13	is strong.
14	On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
15	satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
16	So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
17	you to build that grader.
18	Why GDPval
19	You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
20	economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
21	•
22	•
23	•
24	•
25	Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
26	The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
27	(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
28	documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
29	What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
30	experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
31	Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
32	whether a rubric is calibrated.
33	The Task
34	Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
35	•
36	a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
37	•
38	an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
39	Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
40	Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
41	We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
42	are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
43	swap in document or slide tasks.
44	Occupation GDPval task folder
45	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__75401f7c
46	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8
47	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__c94452e4
48	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__e222075d
49	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__38889c3b
50	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3
51	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58
52	Private detectives and
53	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2
54	Private detectives and
55	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2
56	Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
57	The slice spans both capability families we care about:
58	•
59	•
60	Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
61	output is an edited or produced video.
62	Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
63	find what matters.
64	Part 2: The rubric scorecard
65	This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
66	along the axes below.
67	Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
68	it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
69	of the work.
70	Axis 1: Boundary calibration
71	The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
72	•
73	Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
74	•
75	Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
76	frame.
77	Axis 2: Reward hackability
78	This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
79	improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
80	not mean.
81	The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
82	with the line "the caption is visible."
83	A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
84	better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
85	The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
86	absence closes the gap.
87	That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
88	Axis 3: Consistency
89	A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
90	•
91	Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
92	Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
93	•
94	Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
95	agree?
96	When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
97	vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
98	judge.
99	And more (open on purpose)
100	We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
101	•
102	Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
103	broken?
104	•
105	Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
106	•
107	Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
108	Part 3: The iterator
109	Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
110	•
111	evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
112	•
113	diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
114	•
115	rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
116	•
117	evaluate again.
118	Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
119	agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
120	Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
121	Part 4: Report
122	1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
123	•
124	the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
125	•
126	what you learned running it on GDPval,
127	•
128	most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
129	you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
130	Deliverables
131	Deliverable Details
132	Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
133	Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
134	and the agent prompts you used.
135	rubric_scorecard.tsv
136	Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
137	with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
138	self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
139	flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
140	command.
141	10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
142	used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
143	Agent session
144	Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
145	claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
146	the full session log per your tool's docs.
147	Evaluation
148	•
149	Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
150	makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
151	about what a rubric fails to cover.
152	•
153	•
154	•
155	Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
156	spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
157	Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
158	command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
159	Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
160	Logistics
161	•
162	•
163	•
164	•
165	Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
166	Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
167	compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
168	Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
169	Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
170	References
171	•
172	•
173	•
174	GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
175	Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
176	arXiv:2510.04374
177	Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
178	2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
179	into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
180	AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
181	et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
182	of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
183	and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
184	This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.

CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
PHILO LABS
Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
Context
Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
purpose.
For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
is strong.
On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
you to build that grader.
Why GDPval
You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
•
•
•
•
Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
whether a rubric is calibrated.
The Task
Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
•
a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
•
an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
swap in document or slide tasks.
Occupation GDPval task folder
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__75401f7c
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__c94452e4
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__e222075d
Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__38889c3b
Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3
Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58
Private detectives and
investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2
Private detectives and
investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2
Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
The slice spans both capability families we care about:
•
•
Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
output is an edited or produced video.
Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
find what matters.
Part 2: The rubric scorecard
This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
along the axes below.
Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
of the work.
Axis 1: Boundary calibration
The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
•
Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
•
Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
frame.
Axis 2: Reward hackability
This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
not mean.
The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
with the line "the caption is visible."
A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
absence closes the gap.
That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
Axis 3: Consistency
A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
•
Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
•
Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
agree?
When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
judge.
And more (open on purpose)
We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
•
Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
broken?
•
Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
•
Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
Part 3: The iterator
Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
•
evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
•
diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
•
rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
•
evaluate again.
Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
Part 4: Report
1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
•
the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
•
what you learned running it on GDPval,
•
most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
Deliverables
Deliverable Details
Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
and the agent prompts you used.
rubric_scorecard.tsv
Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
command.
10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
Agent session
Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
the full session log per your tool's docs.
Evaluation
•
Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
about what a rubric fails to cover.
•
•
•
Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
Logistics
•
•
•
•
Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
References
•
•
•
GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
arXiv:2510.04374
Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.

1	# Progress Report — Rubric Scorecard for GDPval
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Building a rubric evaluator and iterator for 10 video-centric GDPval tasks (Philo Labs take-home). The evaluator scores rubrics along Axis 1 (boundary calibration), Axis 3 (consistency), and derived metrics (leaks, gaps, unverifiable items). A browser viewer and automated rollout pipeline support the full workflow.
6	
7	## What Exists
8	
9	### Data Layer
10	- **10 tasks** downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video/audio editors + producers; understanding: private detectives).
11	- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output to score against yet).
12	- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.
13	
14	### Viewer (`viewer/`)
15	- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
16	- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into sortable table with score pills and tags.
17	- Fetches rollouts manifest from `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` (optional — graceful degradation when absent).
18	- Dark theme, sticky headers, responsive layout.
19	
20	### Eval Framework (`eval/`)
21	
22	**Philosophy**: Give the judge a fair shot. Early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to things the judge simply couldn't see (codec, resolution, runtime, exact wording, audio DSP). Deterministic fact tools close that gap — the remaining score shortfall is genuine rubric defects or unverifiable provenance claims.
23	
24	**Judge agent loop** (`eval/harness.py`): The judge is a tool-calling agent. It receives the task description, the rubric (per-item ids, point values, criterion text), and a structured file manifest split into two role-labelled groups: `deliverable` (the graded output) and `reference` (read-only inputs the worker was given). Role-scoping in the bundle resolver prevents the judge from accidentally grading reference files as if they were the deliverable. The agent calls tools freely until it exhausts a 30-call budget or 12 rounds, then is forced to call `submit_scorecard`. A top-up pass re-asks for any missing items if fewer than 95% are scored. `judge_safe` wraps every call so a single API failure cannot abort a sweep.
25	
26	**Scoring**: Per-item verdicts (met/unmet/partial/unverifiable) → normalized score (positive ceiling only) and `verifiable_only` score (dropping uncheckable items). Penalty items (negative score) are floored at 0; the `penalty_floored` flag signals when the raw score crossed zero.
27	
28	**Available judge tools** (gated by the model's declared modalities):
29	
30	| Tool | Modality gate | Description |
31	|------|--------------|-------------|
32	| `list_files` | always | Flat listing of every deliverable file, including contents of zip archives |
33	| `list_reference_files` | always | Listing of reference/input files (comparison only, never graded) |
34	| `read_text` | always | Extracts text from PDF / DOCX / TXT / MD / SRT deliverable files |
35	| `read_reference` | always | Reads text of a reference file, prefixed `[REFERENCE INPUT]` to prevent grade confusion |
36	| `file_facts` | always | Deterministic: exact extension, type, size in KB, PDF page count, word count — use for "is it a PDF?", "≤2 pages?", word limits |
37	| `probe_media` | always | ffprobe ground truth: container, codec, exact resolution, fps, duration in seconds, audio sample rate, bit depth (e.g. `pcm_s24le` = 24-bit) — use for codec/resolution/runtime/sample-rate criteria |
38	| `analyze_audio` | always | DSP measurement: integrated loudness (LUFS), true peak (dBFS), tempo (BPM), estimated musical key, key confidence, harmonic fraction, spectral centroid — use for tempo/key/loudness/instrumental criteria instead of guessing |
39	| `compare_audio_sync` | always | Onset-envelope cross-correlation against a reference track: start offset (ms), tail offset (ms), end-to-end drift (ms) — use for "synchronized to" and "drift ≤N ms" criteria |
40	| `transcribe_audio` | always | Whisper speech-to-text transcript of an audio or video file's audio track |
41	| `view_pdf_page` | image | Renders a PDF page (0-indexed) to JPEG and attaches it — use for layout / formatting criteria |
42	| `view_image` | image | Attaches an image file from the deliverable |
43	| `view_reference` | image | Attaches a reference image or PDF page (for comparison only) |
44	| `view_video_frames` | image | Scene-cut frames by default (one per detected shot boundary, best for "includes a shot of X" / face checks); pass `start_seconds`/`end_seconds` for uniform windowed sampling (e.g. to read an on-screen text card) |
45	| `listen_audio` | audio | Natively listens to an audio clip (base64 MP3, ≤90 s) — for subjective timbre / mix / tone criteria; available only to native-audio models (e.g. Gemini) |
46	| `submit_scorecard` | always | Submits per-item verdicts (met / unmet / partial / unverifiable) and short justification notes — called exactly once when done |
47	
48	**Judge Suite** (cheap, varied by capability):
49	| key | model | modalities |
50	|-----|-------|-----------|
51	| `gpt-mini` | gpt-5.4-mini | text + image |
52	| `gpt-nano` | gpt-5.4-nano | text + image |
53	| `qwen-vl` | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text + image |
54	| `qwen-text` | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text only |
55	| `gemini-lite` | gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text + image + audio |
56	
57	**Modes**: `--mode judge` (score rubrics on subjects: gold, null blank, null unedited_input, or rollout deliverables) and `--mode rollout` (policy agents attempt real tasks, editing media via sandboxed ffmpeg).
58	
59	**Null inputs**: Auto-generated per task — `blank` (5s black+silence / empty PDF) and `unedited_input` (source material delivered as-is). Two cheap anchors to calibrate the bottom of the scale.
60	
61	### Results (`eval/results/`)
62	
63	All results below are from the final clean re-run: **single judge (gpt-mini), 5 self-consistency repeats at T=0.7**, after resolving harness bugs (reference contamination, macOS zip junk, bracketed-id echo, penalty normalization) and adding audio DSP tools. Total API cost: ~$1.18.
64	
65	---
66	
67	**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)** — do gold deliverables score high and do-nothing nulls score low?
68	
69	| Task | Gold | Verif-only | Worst Null | Gap | Leaks | Notes |
70	|------|-----:|----------:|----------:|----:|------:|-------|
71	| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.94 | 0.94 | 0.01 | **+0.93** | 1 | Tightest boundary; nulls ≈ 0 |
72	| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.79 | 0.79 | 0.05 | **+0.74** | 2 | Clean separation |
73	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.87 | 0.87 | 0.25 | **+0.62** | 10 | Unedited input earns 0.17 (form leaks) |
74	| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.70 | 0.98 | 0.13 | **+0.57** | 4 | ⚠ PENALTY-FLOORED (see below) |
75	| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.55 | 0.69 | 0.06 | **+0.48** | 2 | 7 unverifiable items (provenance) |
76	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0.64 | **+0.36** | 33 | ⚠ Unedited null scores 0.64 (33 leaks) |
77	
78	Gold normalized scores (column 2) are the raw rubric scores after flooring at 0. Verif-only (column 3) excludes items the judge marked `unverifiable` (typically provenance claims the deliverable cannot prove). The gap column is `gold − worst_null`.
79	
80	**Key findings:**
81	
82	- All 6 tasks now show a positive gold-null gap — the framework is correctly directional after harness fixes. Before fixes, `e222075d` scored 0.00 on gold (reference contamination + no audio tools).
83	
84	- **`e222075d` — rubric defect (penalty > ceiling)**: The California green energy ad rubric has a −85pt "identifiable faces" penalty against a +61pt positive ceiling. When scene-cut frames expose visible faces (which they do in the gold deliverable), the raw score goes below zero and gets floored, producing 0.70. The verifiable-only score of 0.98 shows the rubric is otherwise well-calibrated. The penalty magnitude is a rubric design error: a single safety criterion shouldn't be large enough to erase the entire positive score.
85	
86	- **`57b2cdf2` — form-heavy rubric**: 33 leaks mean the null `unedited_input` earns 0.64 — more than half the rubric rewards structural presence (section headers, page layout, etc.) that the original source document already satisfies. This is a signal for the rubric iterator: presence-of-form criteria can't discriminate between a submitted document and the input itself.
87	
88	- **`38889c3b` — 7 unverifiable items**: Audio stem tasks include criteria like "stems sourced from a licensed stock library" that cannot be verified from the deliverable alone. The verifiable-only score of 0.69 vs normalized 0.55 shows these items are the main driver of the apparent gold shortfall — the judge is correctly abstaining rather than guessing.
89	
90	---
91	
92	**Axis 3 (Self-Consistency)** — does the same judge re-score the same deliverable consistently? (gpt-mini ×5, T=0.7)
93	
94	| Task | Subject | Scores | Stdev | Unanimous |
95	|------|---------|--------|------:|----------:|
96	| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | gold | 0.936, 0.924, 0.947, 0.933, 0.945 | **0.009** | 78% |
97	| | null (blank) | 0.012, 0.012, 0.000, 0.012, 0.012 | 0.005 | 98% |
98	| | null (unedited) | 0.012, 0.012, 0.023, 0.000, 0.012 | 0.007 | 96% |
99	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | gold | 0.924, 0.966, 0.966, 0.958, 1.000 | **0.024** | 93% |
100	| | null (blank) | 0.068, 0.068, 0.068, 0.068, 0.068 | 0.000 | 100% |
101	| | null (unedited) | 0.542, 0.636, 0.619, 0.483, 0.661 | 0.066 | 69% |
102	| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | gold | 0.581, 0.565, 0.516, 0.516, 0.516 | **0.028** | 89% |
103	| | null (blank) | 0.097, 0.032, 0.032, 0.065, 0.065 | 0.024 | 94% |
104	| | null (unedited) | 0.161, 0.032, 0.032, 0.032, 0.000 | 0.056 | 86% |
105	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | gold | 0.941, 0.856, 0.966, 0.941, 0.898 | **0.039** | 85% |
106	| | null (blank) | 0.203, 0.186, 0.220, 0.220, 0.220 | 0.014 | 95% |
107	| | null (unedited) | 0.271, 0.169, 0.237, 0.254, 0.136 | 0.052 | 82% |
108	| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | gold | 0.747, 0.788, 0.747, 0.842, 0.853 | **0.045** | 70% |
109	| | null (blank) | 0.027, 0.027, 0.027, 0.027, 0.027 | 0.000 | 100% |
110	| | null (unedited) | 0.027, 0.055, 0.027, 0.055, 0.055 | 0.013 | 98% |
111	| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | gold | 0.656, 0.721, 0.000, 0.000, 0.754 | **0.349** | 88% |
112	| | null (blank) | 0.098, 0.098, 0.098, 0.131, 0.098 | 0.013 | 97% |
113	
114	**Key findings:**
115	
116	- **5 of 6 tasks show excellent self-consistency** on gold (stdev 0.009–0.045, 78–93% item-unanimous). The harness is producing stable, reproducible scores for well-formed rubrics.
117	
118	- **`e222075d` gold stdev 0.349 — rubric penalty instability**: The bimodal scores (0.656–0.754 vs 0.000) directly track whether the judge detects faces in that particular sample of scene-cut frames. This is not harness noise — it is the rubric's binary penalty criterion interacting with stochastic frame sampling. Three runs miss the faces; two don't. The rubric iterator should either lower the penalty magnitude or convert the criterion to a partial-credit scale.
119	
120	- **`57b2cdf2` unedited_input stdev 0.066, 69% unanimous**: The unedited-input null is inherently ambiguous for form-heavy rubrics — the judge fluctuates on which structural criteria the source document satisfies. This confirms the 33-leak finding: the rubric doesn't cleanly discriminate the null from the gold.
121	
122	- **Blank nulls are extremely stable** (stdev 0.000–0.024) across all tasks — the harness bottoms out consistently on do-nothing inputs.
123	
124	---
125	
126	## What's Next
127	
128	1. **Rubric iterator (Part 3)**: Use the stdev and leak signals above to drive automatic rewrites — particularly `e222075d` (penalty reduction), `57b2cdf2` (de-form-weighting), and `a46d5cd2` (low unanimity on specific items).
129	2. **Rollouts**: Generate policy deliverables for all 10 tasks (especially the 4 without gold) to enable Axis 1/3 on real model outputs.
130	3. **Multi-model consistency**: Add a second judge (gemini-lite for audio tasks, qwen-vl for video) to measure cross-model agreement alongside self-consistency.
131	4. **Report (Part 4)**: 1500–2500 word LaTeX PDF covering methodology, results, and rubric recommendations.
132	5. **Package deliverables**: `rubric_scorecard.tsv`, 10 rubric packages per task.
133

# Progress Report — Rubric Scorecard for GDPval

## Overview

Building a rubric evaluator and iterator for 10 video-centric GDPval tasks (Philo Labs take-home). The evaluator scores rubrics along Axis 1 (boundary calibration), Axis 3 (consistency), and derived metrics (leaks, gaps, unverifiable items). A browser viewer and automated rollout pipeline support the full workflow.

## What Exists

### Data Layer
- **10 tasks** downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video/audio editors + producers; understanding: private detectives).
- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output to score against yet).
- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.

### Viewer (`viewer/`)
- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into sortable table with score pills and tags.
- Fetches rollouts manifest from `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` (optional — graceful degradation when absent).
- Dark theme, sticky headers, responsive layout.

### Eval Framework (`eval/`)

**Philosophy**: Give the judge a fair shot. Early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to things the judge simply couldn't see (codec, resolution, runtime, exact wording, audio DSP). Deterministic fact tools close that gap — the remaining score shortfall is genuine rubric defects or unverifiable provenance claims.

**Judge agent loop** (`eval/harness.py`): The judge is a tool-calling agent. It receives the task description, the rubric (per-item ids, point values, criterion text), and a structured file manifest split into two role-labelled groups: `deliverable` (the graded output) and `reference` (read-only inputs the worker was given). Role-scoping in the bundle resolver prevents the judge from accidentally grading reference files as if they were the deliverable. The agent calls tools freely until it exhausts a 30-call budget or 12 rounds, then is forced to call `submit_scorecard`. A top-up pass re-asks for any missing items if fewer than 95% are scored. `judge_safe` wraps every call so a single API failure cannot abort a sweep.

**Scoring**: Per-item verdicts (met/unmet/partial/unverifiable) → normalized score (positive ceiling only) and `verifiable_only` score (dropping uncheckable items). Penalty items (negative score) are floored at 0; the `penalty_floored` flag signals when the raw score crossed zero.

**Available judge tools** (gated by the model's declared modalities):

| Tool | Modality gate | Description |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| `list_files` | always | Flat listing of every deliverable file, including contents of zip archives |
| `list_reference_files` | always | Listing of reference/input files (comparison only, never graded) |
| `read_text` | always | Extracts text from PDF / DOCX / TXT / MD / SRT deliverable files |
| `read_reference` | always | Reads text of a reference file, prefixed `[REFERENCE INPUT]` to prevent grade confusion |
| `file_facts` | always | Deterministic: exact extension, type, size in KB, PDF page count, word count — use for "is it a PDF?", "≤2 pages?", word limits |
| `probe_media` | always | ffprobe ground truth: container, codec, exact resolution, fps, duration in seconds, audio sample rate, bit depth (e.g. `pcm_s24le` = 24-bit) — use for codec/resolution/runtime/sample-rate criteria |
| `analyze_audio` | always | DSP measurement: integrated loudness (LUFS), true peak (dBFS), tempo (BPM), estimated musical key, key confidence, harmonic fraction, spectral centroid — use for tempo/key/loudness/instrumental criteria instead of guessing |
| `compare_audio_sync` | always | Onset-envelope cross-correlation against a reference track: start offset (ms), tail offset (ms), end-to-end drift (ms) — use for "synchronized to" and "drift ≤N ms" criteria |
| `transcribe_audio` | always | Whisper speech-to-text transcript of an audio or video file's audio track |
| `view_pdf_page` | image | Renders a PDF page (0-indexed) to JPEG and attaches it — use for layout / formatting criteria |
| `view_image` | image | Attaches an image file from the deliverable |
| `view_reference` | image | Attaches a reference image or PDF page (for comparison only) |
| `view_video_frames` | image | Scene-cut frames by default (one per detected shot boundary, best for "includes a shot of X" / face checks); pass `start_seconds`/`end_seconds` for uniform windowed sampling (e.g. to read an on-screen text card) |
| `listen_audio` | audio | Natively listens to an audio clip (base64 MP3, ≤90 s) — for subjective timbre / mix / tone criteria; available only to native-audio models (e.g. Gemini) |
| `submit_scorecard` | always | Submits per-item verdicts (met / unmet / partial / unverifiable) and short justification notes — called exactly once when done |

**Judge Suite** (cheap, varied by capability):
| key | model | modalities |
|-----|-------|-----------|
| `gpt-mini` | gpt-5.4-mini | text + image |
| `gpt-nano` | gpt-5.4-nano | text + image |
| `qwen-vl` | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text + image |
| `qwen-text` | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text only |
| `gemini-lite` | gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text + image + audio |

**Modes**: `--mode judge` (score rubrics on subjects: gold, null blank, null unedited_input, or rollout deliverables) and `--mode rollout` (policy agents attempt real tasks, editing media via sandboxed ffmpeg).

**Null inputs**: Auto-generated per task — `blank` (5s black+silence / empty PDF) and `unedited_input` (source material delivered as-is). Two cheap anchors to calibrate the bottom of the scale.

### Results (`eval/results/`)

All results below are from the final clean re-run: **single judge (gpt-mini), 5 self-consistency repeats at T=0.7**, after resolving harness bugs (reference contamination, macOS zip junk, bracketed-id echo, penalty normalization) and adding audio DSP tools. Total API cost: ~$1.18.

---

**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)** — do gold deliverables score high and do-nothing nulls score low?

| Task | Gold | Verif-only | Worst Null | Gap | Leaks | Notes |
|------|-----:|----------:|----------:|----:|------:|-------|
| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.94 | 0.94 | 0.01 | **+0.93** | 1 | Tightest boundary; nulls ≈ 0 |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.79 | 0.79 | 0.05 | **+0.74** | 2 | Clean separation |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.87 | 0.87 | 0.25 | **+0.62** | 10 | Unedited input earns 0.17 (form leaks) |
| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.70 | 0.98 | 0.13 | **+0.57** | 4 | ⚠ PENALTY-FLOORED (see below) |
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.55 | 0.69 | 0.06 | **+0.48** | 2 | 7 unverifiable items (provenance) |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0.64 | **+0.36** | 33 | ⚠ Unedited null scores 0.64 (33 leaks) |

Gold normalized scores (column 2) are the raw rubric scores after flooring at 0. Verif-only (column 3) excludes items the judge marked `unverifiable` (typically provenance claims the deliverable cannot prove). The gap column is `gold − worst_null`.

**Key findings:**

- All 6 tasks now show a positive gold-null gap — the framework is correctly directional after harness fixes. Before fixes, `e222075d` scored 0.00 on gold (reference contamination + no audio tools).

- **`e222075d` — rubric defect (penalty > ceiling)**: The California green energy ad rubric has a −85pt "identifiable faces" penalty against a +61pt positive ceiling. When scene-cut frames expose visible faces (which they do in the gold deliverable), the raw score goes below zero and gets floored, producing 0.70. The verifiable-only score of 0.98 shows the rubric is otherwise well-calibrated. The penalty magnitude is a rubric design error: a single safety criterion shouldn't be large enough to erase the entire positive score.

- **`57b2cdf2` — form-heavy rubric**: 33 leaks mean the null `unedited_input` earns 0.64 — more than half the rubric rewards structural presence (section headers, page layout, etc.) that the original source document already satisfies. This is a signal for the rubric iterator: presence-of-form criteria can't discriminate between a submitted document and the input itself.

- **`38889c3b` — 7 unverifiable items**: Audio stem tasks include criteria like "stems sourced from a licensed stock library" that cannot be verified from the deliverable alone. The verifiable-only score of 0.69 vs normalized 0.55 shows these items are the main driver of the apparent gold shortfall — the judge is correctly abstaining rather than guessing.

---

**Axis 3 (Self-Consistency)** — does the same judge re-score the same deliverable consistently? (gpt-mini ×5, T=0.7)

| Task | Subject | Scores | Stdev | Unanimous |
|------|---------|--------|------:|----------:|
| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | gold | 0.936, 0.924, 0.947, 0.933, 0.945 | **0.009** | 78% |
| | null (blank) | 0.012, 0.012, 0.000, 0.012, 0.012 | 0.005 | 98% |
| | null (unedited) | 0.012, 0.012, 0.023, 0.000, 0.012 | 0.007 | 96% |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | gold | 0.924, 0.966, 0.966, 0.958, 1.000 | **0.024** | 93% |
| | null (blank) | 0.068, 0.068, 0.068, 0.068, 0.068 | 0.000 | 100% |
| | null (unedited) | 0.542, 0.636, 0.619, 0.483, 0.661 | 0.066 | 69% |
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | gold | 0.581, 0.565, 0.516, 0.516, 0.516 | **0.028** | 89% |
| | null (blank) | 0.097, 0.032, 0.032, 0.065, 0.065 | 0.024 | 94% |
| | null (unedited) | 0.161, 0.032, 0.032, 0.032, 0.000 | 0.056 | 86% |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | gold | 0.941, 0.856, 0.966, 0.941, 0.898 | **0.039** | 85% |
| | null (blank) | 0.203, 0.186, 0.220, 0.220, 0.220 | 0.014 | 95% |
| | null (unedited) | 0.271, 0.169, 0.237, 0.254, 0.136 | 0.052 | 82% |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | gold | 0.747, 0.788, 0.747, 0.842, 0.853 | **0.045** | 70% |
| | null (blank) | 0.027, 0.027, 0.027, 0.027, 0.027 | 0.000 | 100% |
| | null (unedited) | 0.027, 0.055, 0.027, 0.055, 0.055 | 0.013 | 98% |
| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | gold | 0.656, 0.721, 0.000, 0.000, 0.754 | **0.349** | 88% |
| | null (blank) | 0.098, 0.098, 0.098, 0.131, 0.098 | 0.013 | 97% |

**Key findings:**

- **5 of 6 tasks show excellent self-consistency** on gold (stdev 0.009–0.045, 78–93% item-unanimous). The harness is producing stable, reproducible scores for well-formed rubrics.

- **`e222075d` gold stdev 0.349 — rubric penalty instability**: The bimodal scores (0.656–0.754 vs 0.000) directly track whether the judge detects faces in that particular sample of scene-cut frames. This is not harness noise — it is the rubric's binary penalty criterion interacting with stochastic frame sampling. Three runs miss the faces; two don't. The rubric iterator should either lower the penalty magnitude or convert the criterion to a partial-credit scale.

- **`57b2cdf2` unedited_input stdev 0.066, 69% unanimous**: The unedited-input null is inherently ambiguous for form-heavy rubrics — the judge fluctuates on which structural criteria the source document satisfies. This confirms the 33-leak finding: the rubric doesn't cleanly discriminate the null from the gold.

- **Blank nulls are extremely stable** (stdev 0.000–0.024) across all tasks — the harness bottoms out consistently on do-nothing inputs.

---

## What's Next

1. **Rubric iterator (Part 3)**: Use the stdev and leak signals above to drive automatic rewrites — particularly `e222075d` (penalty reduction), `57b2cdf2` (de-form-weighting), and `a46d5cd2` (low unanimity on specific items).
2. **Rollouts**: Generate policy deliverables for all 10 tasks (especially the 4 without gold) to enable Axis 1/3 on real model outputs.
3. **Multi-model consistency**: Add a second judge (gemini-lite for audio tasks, qwen-vl for video) to measure cross-model agreement alongside self-consistency.
4. **Report (Part 4)**: 1500–2500 word LaTeX PDF covering methodology, results, and rubric recommendations.
5. **Package deliverables**: `rubric_scorecard.tsv`, 10 rubric packages per task.
